---
title: "The Sign Flip, Live"
date: "2026-07-13"
---

# The Sign Flip, Live {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to Chapter four-point-two, the staggered-adoption crisis, part five of thirteen. In part four we built the smallest broken world — two states, a policy that helps in every treated cell with a building effect, no never-treated unit, perfect parallel trends, and a true effect of about minus three-point-five. Now we run the regression on that table, watch the coefficient come out positive, and trace by hand exactly why.
:::

## TWFE Returns the Wrong Sign

- Run $Y_{it}=\alpha_i+\lambda_t+\beta D_{it}+\varepsilon_{it}$ on the 16 observations
- **$\hat\beta=+0.40$** — positive!
- A policy that helped every treated state-year reads as **harmful**
- The sign is not imprecise — it is **wrong**

::: notes
Now run the two-way fixed-effects regression on these sixteen observations. You can do it by hand — it is just two-way demeaning — or trust the arithmetic: the coefficient comes out positive, beta-hat equals plus zero-point-four-zero. A policy that lowered non-renewals in every treated state-year produces a coefficient saying it raised them. The sign is wrong. Not imprecise — wrong. So how can demeaning a table of all-negative effects give a positive number? It is the forbidden comparison from earlier, now caught in the act.
:::

## The Forbidden Comparison, Caught in the Act

- Years 5–8: to estimate L, TWFE needs a control that didn't change
- Only candidate is State E — but E is **already treated** there
- E's effect is **still deepening**, $-4$ down to $-7$
- E slides down **faster** than freshly-treated L

::: notes
Focus on years five through eight, when State L is treated. To estimate L's effect, two-way fixed effects wants a control — units whose status did not change when L switched on. With no never-treated state, the only candidate is State E, already treated throughout years five to eight. So the regression must use already-treated State E as the control for newly-treated State L. And what is E doing? Its effect is still deepening, dropping from minus four to minus seven — E's outcome is falling fast for reasons that have nothing to do with L. It is E's own dynamic effect, still in motion.
:::

## Why the Difference Flips Positive

- TWFE differences L's change against E's change over years 5–8
- E is **not** flat — it slides **downward**, faster than L
- So L looks like it is **rising relative to E**
- The forbidden comparison subtracts E's still-growing effect from L's

::: notes
When two-way fixed effects differences L's change against E's change over years five through eight, E is not a flat baseline — it is sliding downward under its own still-growing effect, faster than freshly-treated L. So L's outcome looks like it is rising relative to E, even though L's own non-renewals are falling. The forbidden comparison subtracts E's still-growing effect from L's, and because E moves the same direction but faster, the differenced quantity for L flips positive. With no clean control to dilute it, that comparison dominates the pooled average, and beta-hat comes out the wrong sign.
:::

## Add One Clean State and It's Contained

- With **no** never-treated unit, forbidden comparison is the only game in town
- That is what delivers the **full** sign flip
- Add one never-treated state: TWFE stays negative but **attenuated**
- $\approx -1.2$ vs true $-2.6$ — clean comparisons keep the **sign**

::: notes
One more crucial point. Add even one never-treated state and the catastrophe is contained. Two-way fixed effects stays negative but badly attenuated — around minus one-point-two instead of the true minus two-point-six on a six-year version. The clean comparisons the never-treated state now provides keep the sign right. The full sign flip needs the forbidden comparisons to be the only game in town, which is exactly what no never-treated unit delivers. Even with a clean control present, the magnitude is badly wrong — attenuated toward zero by the contamination that remains.
:::

## The Whole Crisis in One Table

- TWFE used **already-treated E** as a control for **newly-treated L**
- E's own effect was **still changing**
- That changing effect got **subtracted** from the thing you wanted to measure
- Everything that follows is a precise accounting of when and how badly

::: notes
That is the entire crisis in one table. Hold the mechanism: two-way fixed effects used an already-treated unit, State E, whose own effect was still changing, as a control for a newly-treated unit, State L, and that changing effect got subtracted from the thing you were trying to measure. Everything that follows is a precise accounting of when and how badly this happens — first Goodman-Bacon, then de Chaisemartin and D'Haultfœuille's negative-weights result, and finally the estimators that route around the whole problem.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
On data where the policy helps in **every** cell (truth $\approx -3.5$), TWFE returns **$+0.40$** — the sign flip, live. Cause: with no never-treated unit, the **only** control for late-adopting L is already-treated E, whose still-deepening effect gets subtracted out. Add one clean state and the sign survives but is badly **attenuated**. Next: Goodman-Bacon names every culprit exactly.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part five: on a table where disclosure helps in every treated cell, with perfect parallel trends, the coefficient comes out positive — plus zero-point-four-zero — when the truth is about minus three-point-five. With no never-treated unit, the only control for late-adopting L is already-treated E, whose still-deepening effect gets subtracted from L's, flipping the sign. Add one clean state and the sign survives but the magnitude is badly attenuated. In part six the Goodman-Bacon decomposition proves this is no accident: the coefficient is exactly a weighted average of every possible two-by-two, and it names which ones are the poison.
:::
